narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your undergraduate GPA of 3.20, an LSAT score of 27, middle-family income background, and plans to attend full-time, along with your school decile rankings from Year 1 and Year 3 both at the 1st decile—meaning your early grades placed you at the lower end compared to your peers, well below the typical 6th decile averages. The admissions model, trained on thousands of similar students' paths to the bar exam, predicted a 90.8% chance that you would fail the bar on your first try, which directly led to the rejection of your application since the school prioritizes applicants likely to pass and succeed in practice. This high failure risk stems from a few key aspects of your profile that carried the strongest weight in the model's decision. Most influential was your Year 3 school decile ranking in the bottom 1st, which significantly pushed the prediction toward expecting bar failure because lower rankings like yours often correlate with struggles on the exam compared to students in higher deciles. Next, your Year 1 decile at the 1st level reinforced this concern, again steering the model toward failure by highlighting early academic challenges far below the average. Your LSAT score of 27, notably lower than the typical 37, added further influence in the same direction, as weaker scores like this have historically linked to bar exam difficulties. While your undergrad GPA sat close to the average at 3.20 and your full-time status aligns with most applicants, these weren't enough to offset the top concerns. In contrast to typical students who pass the bar—often with mid-to-high school deciles and stronger LSATs—your profile clusters more with those who face first-try failures. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background, but a data-driven flag from patterns in past outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, seeking academic support to improve your decile rankings if possible, or exploring programs with bar prep resources tailored for students like you from Hispanic backgrounds. Building a stronger narrative around your resilience and any upward trends could also help in reapplying or appealing to schools with holistic reviews. With targeted steps, you can shift these odds in your favor.
